text:
Matty Johns has taken a cheeky swipe at Karl Stefanovic after a storm hit Paris, leaving the NRL great and his fellow podcast hosts sitting in soaked shirts as they delivered Olympics coverage.

Commentator and NRL great Matty Johns has taken a sly dig at Karl Stefanovic and other news outlets after rain bucketed down in Paris during their coverage of the Olympics.

Johns and Aussie swimming legend James Magnussen have teamed up to deliver daily updates of the Olympic Games with their podcast Matty and the Missile in Paris.

While recording their latest episode on Thursday night, the sporting legends got caught in a snap downpour of rain which drenched the French capital.

The commentators, who were joined by News Corp sports journalist Peter Badel, hilariously boasted about braving the storm as they sat in soaked shirts to continue the podcast.

"The reason why we are soaked, as we got about seven or eight minutes in to the podcast and it absolutely started hammering down," Johns said.

"We're in the middle of a storm, don't worry we hung in there as long as we could."

Johns took a cheeky swipe at other media agencies, including Nine's Today show host Stefanovic who has been leading the broadcaster's coverage in Paris with co-presenter Sarah Abo.

"(To) give an idea what we put up with, and why you should respect us so much, we saw CBS, NBC, Karl Stefanovic - they all just disappeared into the distance," Johns joked.

"We just hung in there as long as we could."

Johns said despite the efforts of French soldiers attempting to usher them out of the rain, the podcast hosts were determined to "stay the distance".

"I just want to do an apology, I'm wearing a clear white shirt so my nipples are exposed," Johns laughed.

"You always wanted to be in a wet t-shirt competition didn't you, Matty?" Badel amusingly replied.

It has been raining on and off in Paris since theOlympics Opening Ceremony last week, with hundreds of thousands of spectatorsbraving the wet to catch a glimpse of the proceedings.

Crowds of French locals, tourists and delegates lined the quays and banks of the River Seine under the overcast conditions to cheer on this year's Olympic athletes.

Competitors were as thrilled as ever to compete at the international sporting event despite the trying conditions as they flowed down the river on boats.

After the six-kilometre parade along the Seine, athletes filed into grandstand-style seating next to the Eiffel Tower for the rest of the proceedings.
